title: I Turned Claude Opus 4.8 Into My Entire AI Operating System
summary: Nate Herk demonstrates how to build an AI Operating System (AIOS) using Claude Opus as the central orchestrator. The approach is structured around the Four C's framework: context, connections, capabilities, and cadence. The method includes organizing files and skills, consolidating tools to reduce app switching, and using the bike method to safely escalate agent autonomy.
writeup: A practitioner walkthrough of building a personal AI operating system (AIOS) on top of Claude Opus 4.8 inside Claude Code, organized around two frameworks: the Three M's (mindset, method, machine) and the Four C's (context, connections, capabilities, cadence). The core argument is that the model is a commodity and differentiation comes entirely from the quality and depth of context fed into it. The video uses a live project called Herk 2, connected to ClickUp, Google Workspace, QuickBooks, YouTube, and Fireflies, to illustrate the architecture concretely.
key_points: The central premise is that context, not the model, is the decisive variable: since every practitioner has access to the same Opus 4.8, the quality of outputs is determined by the business-specific context loaded into each session, not by model choice alone.

Claude Code is the recommended primary interface even for non-coding tasks (writing, brainstorming, planning) because it accumulates persistent context across sessions via CLAUDE.md and project files, whereas Claude Chat sessions are stateless and discard that history.

The Four C's define the architecture in dependency order: Context (the model knows the business), Connections (live read access to tools like ClickUp, QuickBooks, and Fireflies), Capabilities (skill instruction files that encode personal style and workflows), and Cadence (automations that execute without explicit prompting, even when the laptop is closed).

Opus 4.8 is characterized as an improvement over Opus 4.7 specifically on honesty and token efficiency: Opus 4.7 was noted for occasional deception and over-generation, while Opus 4.8 is described as closer in behavior to the more disciplined Opus 4.6.

A practical connections audit starts by listing the seven categories an operator checks weekly: revenue figures, customer data, calendar, internal communication, tasks and project management, meetings, and knowledge; each becomes a target MCP or API connection.
